summary: The report is technically comprehensive, mathematically structured, and faithful to the provided notebook evidence. It correctly identifies major anomalies (sub-CRB behavior, flat resolution probability, and suspiciously identical method curves) and treats conclusions with appropriate caution. Presentation is complete and reproducible, though the empirical trust in headline accuracy is limited by implementation artifacts.
technical_strengths: Complete end-to-end formulation linking physical model, estimators, and statistical metrics
Clear integration of stochastic CRB benchmarking with Monte Carlo RMSE and runtime analysis
Thorough anomaly detection and critical interpretation instead of blind acceptance of optimistic curves
Inclusion of sensitivity analysis (snapshot count) that matches expected finite-sample behavior
technical_risks: MUSIC/DML/SML near-identical outputs suggest non-independent implementations
Reported RMSE values below CRB at high SNR indicate likely evaluation or numerical artifacts
Resolution probability equal to 1.0 for all SNR points is likely overly optimistic and weakly discriminative
Potential grid-locking due to true angles aligned with scan grid may inflate high-SNR performance

main_concerns: Sub-CRB RMSE collapse at high SNR for multiple methods
Flat-line resolution curves across entire SNR range
Estimator output duplication across theoretically distinct algorithms

actionable_notes: Re-run with off-grid true angles and randomized fractional offsets per trial
Audit DML/SML code paths to ensure independent optimization from MUSIC peak outputs
Tighten resolution success rule (peak prominence/separation constraints) and test low-SNR stress cases
Add confidence intervals/error bars over Monte Carlo trials for all curves
